title: Lightweight Cornhole Boards
summary: A regulation-size cornhole set built with 1x3 frames and 1/2" plywood instead of 2x4s and 3/4" — half the weight, so it actually gets carried to the yard and used.
description:
Traditional cornhole boards are framed in 2x4 with 3/4" plywood tops, and they're boat anchors — 40-plus pounds a pair, which is why they live in the garage instead of getting played. This version swaps the frame to 1x3 (furring strips if you're on a budget) and the deck to 1/2" exterior plywood. The boards still measure the regulation 24" x 48" with a 6" hole; they just don't fight you on the way to the yard.

The legs fold, pivoting on 1/4" bolts with fender washers on both sides and locking nuts. The locking nuts are the detail that matters: a plain nut either works loose (flopping legs) or gets cranked tight (legs won't fold). A nylon-insert lock nut lets you set the pivot friction once and it stays put.

Buy plywood with one smooth face (AC or sanded) — bean bags need to slide, and the difference between a sanded face and sheathing-grade is the difference between a game and an argument. If you paint the decks, finish with a coat or two of water-based poly so the surface is slick and consistent.

The quantities here build a set of two boards; the source doubles everything for a four-board set.

tools.note: With a 1/4" bit for the leg pivot bolts.
The 6" bag hole and the rounded leg tops.
The deck must be smooth or bags won't slide.
1/4" bit for bolt holes, plus a starter hole inside the bag-hole circle.
For the 10° leg cut — a marked triangle and a jigsaw work fine without it.

materials.name: 1x3, 8 ft
materials.note: Frames and legs. Furring strips are the budget option — pick the straight ones.

materials.name: Exterior plywood, 1/2", 48" x 48", one smooth face
materials.note: AC or sanded grade. Two 24" x 48" project panels also work.

materials.name: Exterior deck screws, 1-5/8", star drive, flat head
materials.note: Flat heads sit flush in the deck.

materials.name: Nylon lock nuts, 1/4"
materials.note: Lock nuts, specifically — they hold the pivot friction where you set it.

materials.name: Fender washers, 1/4"
materials.note: One each side of every pivot.

materials.name: Bean bags, set of 8
materials.note: Four per color, two colors. Regulation is 6", ~1 lb.

cutList.note: One end cut at 10° off square (long point measurement), top corners rounded for the fold.
A 48x48 sheet cut exactly in half.

steps.title: Build the two frames
steps.body:
Lay out each frame on a flat surface — two 48" sides, an end at each end, and a brace across the middle — and screw the sides into the crosspieces, two screws per joint.

1x3 is thin stock near an end: if a screw starts to split it, stop and pre-drill the rest. A split frame member now is a wobbly board forever.
materials: Exterior deck screws, 1-5/8", star drive, flat head

steps.title: Drill the leg pivot holes in the frames
steps.body:
Mark the pivot centers near the top corners of each frame and drill through with the 1/4" bit.

Press a screw tip into the mark first to make a small dent — it keeps the bit from walking, and a pivot hole that wanders even 1/8" gives you a board that sits crooked on its legs.

steps.title: Deck the frames and cut the hole
steps.body:
Glue and screw each plywood deck to its frame — about a dozen screws around the perimeter, heads pulled flush.

Mark the bag hole: 6" diameter, centered side-to-side, 9" from the top edge to the hole's center. Drill a 1/4" starter hole just inside the line, then cut the circle with the jigsaw, staying on the waste side. Sand the hole edge smooth and round it slightly — bags catch on square edges.
materials: Exterior plywood, 1/2", 48" x 48", one smooth face
Exterior deck screws, 1-5/8", star drive, flat head

steps.title: Make the folding legs
steps.body:
Cut the legs with a 10° angle at the foot end — that's what makes the board sit at regulation height with the foot flat on the grass. No miter saw? Mark 1/2" up from one end, draw the triangle back to the long edge, and cut it with a jigsaw or circular saw.

Round the top corners off each leg so it can rotate inside the frame, and drill the 1/4" pivot hole through the rounded end.

steps.title: Bolt the legs on and test the fold
steps.body:
Stack the hardware in this order: bolt head | fender washer | frame side | leg | fender washer | lock nut. Snug the lock nut until the leg swings smoothly but holds its position.

Fold and unfold each leg a few times, then stand both boards 27 feet apart, front edge to front edge, and confirm they sit level. Sand the decks, paint if you like — and topcoat with poly so the bags slide the same on both boards.

images.alt: Pair of lightweight wooden cornhole boards with folding legs set up on a lawn
